title: The Subject of AI: A Psychoanalytic Intervention
author: Black and Johanssen

opening_move.description: Existing psychoanalytic approaches to AI (especially Lacanian ones) err by attributing a form of subjective formation to AI itself, treating the chatbot as an 'automated subject'. The paper argues instead that psychoanalysis should illuminate the human-AI relation, not anthropomorphize the machine.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A Lacanian analysis of the user-chatbot relation as inherently relational — rather than AI-as-subject — reveals the social structures and fantasies underpinning human engagement with generative AI.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper works within the Lacanian tradition but corrects what it sees as a misapplication: rather than psychoanalyzing AI as a subject, it uses Lacanian concepts (big Other, five discourses, analyst-analysand relation) to analyze the relational dynamic between human users and chatbots.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Conceptual analysis using Lacanian theory; illustrative interpretation of chatbot interactions (Meta's Liv chatbot, ChatGPT exchanges) as cases to apply the framework.
epistemic_mode.notes: Chatbot exchanges are used illustratively, not as systematic empirical data. The argument is fundamentally conceptual-theoretical.

case_type.case_name: ChatGPT and Meta's Liv chatbot (specific user-chatbot exchanges)

confidence_notes: The paper is a conceptual intervention within the Lacanian psychoanalytic tradition applied to AI. The chatbot exchanges are illustrative cases rather than empirical research. The normative claim is implicit: anthropomorphizing AI as subject is theoretically mistaken and ethically misleading; a relational model is preferable.
